content_en:
Abu Nu'aym Ahmad ibn Abdullah ibn Ahmad ibn Ishaq ibn Musa ibn Mihran al-Isfahani was born in 336 AH (948 CE) in Isfahan (present-day Iran). He was one of the most prolific hadith scholars and transmitters of his era and produced works across the fields of hadith, biography, and Islamic spirituality. He lived to the remarkable age of nearly one hundred, dying in 430 AH (1038 CE), and used this long life to accumulate and transmit an enormous hadith collection.

Isfahani in Abu Nu'aym's era was a significant center of Islamic learning. The Buyid dynasty had revitalized cultural life in the eastern Islamic lands, and Islamic scholarship was flourishing in numerous disciplines simultaneously. Abu Nu'aym studied with many scholars in Isfahan and traveled to other centers of learning, particularly Nishapur, which was at the height of its influence as an intellectual center during this period.

Abu Nu'aym was deeply interested in the tradition of Islamic spirituality — the piety, devotional practices, and inner dimensions of religious life that were increasingly being articulated as a distinctive tradition, which would eventually be called Sufism. His Hilyat al-Awliya' — "Adornment of the Friends of God" — was his major contribution to this literature: a biographical collection documenting the lives of the greatest exemplars of Islamic piety from the Prophet's Companions through the Sufi masters of his own era.

The historical context is important: Abu Nu'aym was writing at a moment when the tradition of Islamic spirituality was being systematized and its relationship to the hadith tradition was being worked out. His approach — grounding the Sufi tradition firmly in hadith documentation, presenting each figure with transmitted accounts from reliable sources — was a deliberate strategy to demonstrate the legitimacy of this tradition within mainstream Islamic scholarship. The Hilyat al-Awliya' was thus both a biographical work and a defense of Islamic spirituality as an authentic dimension of prophetic teaching.
content_id:
Abu Nu'aim Ahmad ibn Abdullah ibn Ahmad ibn Ishaq ibn Musa ibn Mihran al-Isfahani lahir pada tahun 336 H (948 M) di Isfahan (sekarang Iran). Ia adalah salah satu ulama hadis dan perawi paling produktif zamannya dan menghasilkan karya-karya di bidang hadis, biografi, dan spiritualitas Islam. Ia hidup hingga usia yang luar biasa hampir seratus tahun, wafat pada tahun 430 H (1038 M), dan menggunakan umur panjang ini untuk mengumpulkan dan meriwayatkan koleksi hadis yang sangat besar.

Isfahan pada era Abu Nu'aim adalah pusat pembelajaran Islam yang signifikan. Dinasti Buwaihi telah merevitalisasi kehidupan budaya di tanah-tanah Islam bagian timur, dan ilmu Islam sedang berkembang pesat dalam banyak disiplin secara bersamaan. Abu Nu'aim belajar dengan banyak ulama di Isfahan dan bepergian ke pusat-pusat pembelajaran lainnya, khususnya Nishapur, yang pada periode ini berada di puncak pengaruhnya sebagai pusat intelektual.

Abu Nu'aim sangat tertarik pada tradisi spiritualitas Islam — kesalehan, praktik-praktik ibadah, dan dimensi-dimensi batin kehidupan beragama yang semakin banyak diartikulasikan sebagai tradisi yang khas, yang akhirnya akan disebut Tasawuf. Hilyat al-Awliya — Perhiasan Para Wali Allah — adalah kontribusi utamanya pada literatur ini: koleksi biografi yang mendokumentasikan kehidupan para teladan terbesar kesalehan Islam dari para Sahabat Nabi hingga guru-guru sufi di eranya sendiri.

Konteks historis sangat penting: Abu Nu'aim menulis pada saat tradisi spiritualitas Islam sedang disistematisasi dan hubungannya dengan tradisi hadis sedang dirumuskan. Pendekatannya — memijak tradisi sufi dengan kuat dalam dokumentasi hadis, menyajikan setiap tokoh dengan riwayat-riwayat yang diterima dari sumber-sumber yang dapat dipercaya — adalah strategi yang disengaja untuk menunjukkan legitimasi tradisi ini dalam ilmu Islam arus utama. Hilyat al-Awliya dengan demikian adalah sekaligus karya biografi dan pembelaan spiritualitas Islam sebagai dimensi autentik ajaran kenabian.
content_ar:
Abu Nu'aym Ahmad ibn Abdullah ibn Ahmad ibn Ishaq ibn Musa ibn Mihran al-Isfahani was born in 336 AH (948 CE) in Isfahan (present-day Iran). He was one of the most prolific الحديث scholars and transmitters of his era and produced works across the fields of الحديث, biography, and Islamic spirituality. He lived to the remarkable age of nearly one hundred, dying in 430 AH (1038 CE), and used this long life to accumulate and transmit an enormous الحديث collection.

Isfahani in Abu Nu'aym's era was a significant center of Islamic learning. The Buyid dynasty had revitalized cultural life in the eastern Islamic lands, and Islamic scholarship was flourishing in numerous disciplines simultaneously. Abu Nu'aym studied with many scholars in Isfahan and traveled to other centers of learning, particularly Nishapur, which was at the height of its influence as an intellectual center during this period.

Abu Nu'aym was deeply interested in the tradition of Islamic spirituality — the piety, devotional practices, and inner dimensions of religious life that were increasingly being articulated as a distinctive tradition, which would eventually be called Sufism. His Hilyat al-Awliya' — "Adornment of the Friends of God" — was his major contribution to this literature: a biographical collection documenting the lives of the greatest exemplars of Islamic piety from النبي ﷺ's الصحابة through the Sufi masters of his own era.

The historical context is important: Abu Nu'aym was writing at a moment when the tradition of Islamic spirituality was being systematized and its relationship to the الحديث tradition was being worked out. His approach — grounding the Sufi tradition firmly in الحديث documentation, presenting each figure with transmitted accounts from reliable sources — was a deliberate strategy to demonstrate the legitimacy of this tradition within mainstream Islamic scholarship. The Hilyat al-Awliya' was thus both a biographical work and a defense of Islamic spirituality as an authentic dimension of prophetic teaching.
